title: Infleqtion receives $2M army contract for navigation technology
content:
Infleqtion announced it secured a $2 million contract from the U.S. Army to develop navigation and timing technology for military applications. The 18-month Direct-to-Phase II Small Business Innovation Research contract will fund development of the company’s Secured AI for Positioning at the Edge, Navigation, and Timing system.

The Boulder, Colorado-based quantum technology company will apply its Contextual Machine Learning technology to enhance the Army’s navigation capabilities in environments where signals may be disrupted or compromised. The system is designed to integrate data from multiple sources including GPS, inertial navigation systems, and computer vision on compact edge computing platforms.

The contract follows Infleqtion’s first-place finish in the Army’s xTechScalable AI Challenge, where the company’s solution was selected from 133 initial submissions. The competition ran from December 2023 to July 2024 and led to this direct Phase II award, which will fund development through October 2026.

"SAPIENT builds on the early promise of CML by applying it to some of the military’s most urgent edge-AI challenges," said Pranav Gokhale, Chief Technology Officer at Infleqtion. "We’re honored to continue working with the Army to develop scalable solutions that enhance operational resilience in the presence of adversarial threats."

Infleqtion develops quantum computers, precision sensors, and quantum software for government and commercial customers. The company announced plans to go public through a merger with Churchill Capital Corp X (CCCX). The technology uses quantum-inspired algorithms and operates on NVIDIA’s CUDA-Q platform.

The company has previously demonstrated applications of its Contextual Machine Learning technology in Army and Navy programs, including RF signal processing applications. The new contract represents an expansion of the technology’s use in multi-sensor environments for military navigation systems.

title: RedCloud reports H1 growth, targets $100M revenue for 2026
content:
RedCloud Holdings plc (RCT) reported first-half 2025 results showing revenue of $17.9 million, up 12% year-over-year, and total transaction value of $1.2 billion, representing 28% growth compared to the same period in 2024.

The London-based technology company, which builds infrastructure for global trade through its RedAI platform, reduced total liabilities to $21.4 million from $86.3 million in the prior year period. Connected businesses on the platform increased 110% to 68,091, while distributors grew 136% to 1,018.

For full year 2025, RedCloud expects revenue between $51 million and $53 million. The company has set a target of $100 million in revenue for 2026.

"We doubled our data capture. We launched in Saudi Arabia. We joined NVIDIA Connect," said CEO and Co-Founder Justin Floyd. "We’re targeting $100 million revenues next year."

RedCloud signed an infrastructure license agreement in Turkey that provides up to $5 million per year in license fees with a minimum 10-year term, plus 50% revenue share on transactional revenue. The company also launched a joint venture in Saudi Arabia with Kayanat Holding to enter the Kingdom’s consumer goods market.

The company joined NVIDIA’s Connect partner program and reports $5.1 billion in cumulative trades training its algorithms. RedCloud converted $66.9 million of shareholder loans to equity and eliminated $22.6 million of convertible debt. Additional equity raises totaling $16.6 million were completed in July, along with securing a £2 million revolving credit facility.

RedCloud will host an earnings conference call on December 11 at 8:30 a.m. Eastern Time.

title: Lockheed Martin launches AI platform for government agencies
content:
Lockheed Martin (LMT) announced the launch of Astris AI for Government, a platform designed to provide artificial intelligence solutions for federal agencies and high-assurance applications. The initiative comes through Astris AI, a wholly owned subsidiary of Lockheed Martin.

The platform integrates technology from multiple companies into a unified system that government agencies can use to build, deploy and maintain AI applications. The solution combines Oracle Cloud Infrastructure’s secure network, Lockheed Martin’s engineering capabilities, open source AI models from Meta, and NVIDIA AI Enterprise software.

"Our Astris AI for Government initiative was created to help advance America’s AI Action Plan, ensuring American leadership in AI diplomacy and security, while achieving domestic priorities for acquisition reform," said Sarah Hiza, senior vice president of Technology & Strategic Innovation at Lockheed Martin.

The platform enables agencies to process mission-critical data on-site while maintaining security protocols. Federal agencies can use the system to analyze information without moving sensitive data off their networks through machine learning operations and generative AI capabilities.

Potential applications include wildfire response support for the Department of the Interior, energy infrastructure protection, and supply chain optimization across federal government operations. The system allows first responders to access real-time intelligence by combining sensor data from multiple sources.

The Astris AI for Government solutions are available through Astris AI, Oracle Marketplace and federal procurement channels. Initial offerings include the Astris AI Government platform on Oracle Cloud Infrastructure, featuring generative AI and machine learning operations capabilities powered by NVIDIA AI Enterprise and NVIDIA NIM microservices.

title: BofA reaffirms this AI-chip giant as its top pick, citing decade-long tech lead
content:
Investing.com -- Bank of America reiterated a well-known semiconductor name as its top pick in a note Thursday, maintaining a Buy rating and a $275 price target on the stock, after what it called a “positive” investor relations meeting.

Nvidia is the stock in question. BofA analyst Vivek Arya wrote that “NVDA GPUs remain a full generation ahead of competition,” noting that today’s leading GPU-trained large language models are still based on 2022’s Hopper architecture, while the upcoming Blackwell generation is “10x–15x better gen-over-gen.”

Blackwell-trained LLMs are expected to arrive in early 2026.

Nvidia is also said to have told investors that its next-generation Vera Rubin platform remains on schedule for the second half of 2026, with a pre-fill inference CPX version coming in the fourth quarter of next year.

Arya highlighted Nvidia’s customer strength, writing that “Google remains a key and growing customer, for NVDA every model builder still runs on NVDA.”

Crucially, BofA said Nvidia has both demand and supply visibility into “the $500bn sales outlook in CY25–26 at the minimum,” adding that new partnerships with OpenAI and Anthropic are incremental sources of upside.

Nvidia also stressed that even five-year-old Ampere GPUs remain “nearly 100% utilized,” supporting its view that a 5–6 year GPU life cycle is appropriate.

The note emphasised Nvidia’s unmatched ecosystem, citing its “end-to-end platform… something no others can replicate.” Despite rising memory costs, the firm said Nvidia’s “mid-70% GM outlook remains unchanged.”

Bank of America argued that Nvidia’s valuation is “compelling”, equating to a PEG ratio of just 0.5x.

title: Nvidia, AI stocks fall after Oracle earnings underwhelm
content:
Investing.com-- Nvidia and other stocks tied to the artificial intelligence trade slipped on Thursday, weighed down by weak earnings and a high outlook for capital expenditures from cloud-computing giant Oracle which sparked renewed concerns over stretched AI spending.

Nvidia (NASDAQ:NVDA), which has major contracts in place to supply Oracle (NYSE:ORCL) with data center chips, fell 1.3%, while rival cloud firm CoreWeave (NASDAQ:CRWV) fell over 3%. Both companies, and Oracle, have deals to supply AI startup OpenAI.

Other AI server and chip firms, including Broadcom Inc (NASDAQ:AVGO), Marvell Technology Inc (NASDAQ:MRVL), and AMD (NASDAQ:AMD), fell between 0.5% and 1%.

In Europe, chipmakers like Infineon, STMicro, ASML, and ASM were also hovering below the flatline in mid-morning dealmaking.

Shares of Oracle slid over 11% in premarket U.S. trading after the cloud giant guided for weaker than expected earnings for the current quarter. Oracle’s remaining performance obligation, a gauge of bookings, also came in at $523 billion, slightly below analyst estimates.

Executives also flagged that capital expenditures for fiscal 2026 will be $50 billion, more than earlier forecasts of $35 billion. The weak guidance and high expense outlook added to already high concerns over Oracle’s rising debt levels, with the company seen taking on outsized amounts of debt to fund its AI infrastructure ambitions.

CEO Clay Magouyrk fielded questions on the company’s financing plans during a post-earnings call, but did not offer specifics on how it planned to fund its data center buildout.

"“Cloud growth at the low-end of the guide with building pressure on gross margins and operating margins may further sap investor confidence in Oracle’s ability to execute efficiently against a large and growing book of GPU as a service business, leaving the shares lacking a clear catalyst,” analysts at Morgan Stanley said in a note.

Oracle has sold tens of billions of dollars of bonds in recent months to fund its AI plans, raising concerns over its debt risk. Last week, the cost of protecting Oracle’s debt against default reached its highest level since March 2009.

Along with being a database company, Oracle has found success in its cloud operations over the past two years, signing contracts to supply computing power to several major AI players, including ChatGPT-maker OpenAI.

Wednesday’s earnings, however, threatened to exacerbate concerns over just how quickly Oracle will be able to turn its outsized spending plans into returns for investors. Increased skepticism over OpenAI’s prospects, amid heavy competition from companies such as Alphabet’s (NASDAQ:GOOGL) Google, have also weighed on Oracle in recent weeks.

(Scott Kanowsky contributed reporting.)

title: Wall Street futures slide as Oracle’s forecast revives AI bubble fears
content:
Dec 11 (Reuters) - U.S. stock index futures tumbled on Thursday after Oracle’s forecasts raised fresh concerns about hefty AI spending and outweighed optimism following a less hawkish tone from the Federal Reserve.

Oracle (N:ORCL) slumped 12% in premarket trading after its quarterly forecasts missed analysts’ estimates and the company said annual spending would rise by $15 billion compared with its earlier expectations.

The cloud company had gained prominence earlier this year after announcing deals to build AI cloud data centers for OpenAI.

However, its shares were on track for their biggest quarterly loss since mid-2002 on worries that heavy reliance on debt financing could be a recipe for a bubble similar to the early 2000s.

"Oracle has been at the epicentre of the AI financing debate, lacking the mammoth cash flows of the more traditional cloud giants," said Matt Britzman, senior equity analyst at Hargreaves Lansdown.

Shares of other artificial intelligence companies were also hit.

Chipmakers Nvidia and Broadcom fell 1.6% and 1.5%, respectively, while hyperscalers such as Microsoft and Amazon.com dropped 1% each and CoreWeave declined 3.3%.

At 04:30 a.m. ET, Dow E-minis were down 144 points, or 0.30%, S&P 500 E-minis were down 42.5 points, or 0.62% and Nasdaq 100 E-minis were down 207.5 points, or 0.80%.

Wall Street’s fear gauge, the CBOE volatility index, climbed 0.71 points and was last at 16.47.

Meanwhile, the Federal Reserve lowered borrowing costs by 25 basis points as expected on Wednesday, and Chair Jerome Powell signaled a pause on further easing as the central bank waits for more data on inflation and employment.

However, traders are already pricing in at least 50 bps of monetary easing next year on expectations that U.S. President Donald Trump’s appointee to the Fed Chair will likely be a policy dove. White House economic advisor Kevin Hassett is the front-runner for the job.

"We’re not surprised to see near-term optimism in the markets given that the Fed continues to cut rates even though the economy is growing," said Chris Zaccarelli, chief investment officer at Northlight Asset Management.

"However, we think the rose-colored glasses may come off once investors realize that the path to lower interest rates may take longer – or may not materialize at all – to the extent that they believe it will."

title: Oracle stock price target maintained at $400 by Mizuho after mixed earnings
content:
Investing.com - Mizuho has reiterated its Outperform rating and $400.00 price target on Oracle (NYSE:ORCL) following the company’s mixed fiscal second-quarter results. This target represents nearly 80% upside from the current price of $223.01, significantly above the average analyst consensus for the prominent software player with a market capitalization of $637.69 billion.

Oracle reported a modest revenue miss in its latest quarter, primarily driven by weakness in its license business, though its Cloud segment performance remained relatively in line with expectations. The company has maintained 9.67% revenue growth over the last twelve months, reaching $59.02 billion in total revenue.

In other recent news, Oracle Corporation reported a strong financial performance in its Q2 2026 earnings, with non-GAAP earnings per share (EPS) reaching $2.26, significantly surpassing the forecast of $1.64. This marks a surprise of 37.8%, although there was a slight revenue miss. Jefferies maintained its Buy rating on Oracle, setting a price target of $400, highlighting an "asymmetric risk/reward" despite the mixed quarterly results. Oracle’s backlog stood at $523 billion, slightly ahead of expectations, with Infrastructure-as-a-Service growth of 66% year-over-year, which was below the anticipated 71%. Bernstein lowered its price target to $339 from $364 while maintaining an Outperform rating, noting increased capital expenditures. BofA Securities also reduced its price target to $300 from $368, citing a timing mismatch between AI infrastructure expenses and revenue conversion. Citizens analyst reiterated a Market Outperform rating with a price target of $342, emphasizing Oracle’s "chip neutral" strategy. These developments reflect a varied analyst perspective on Oracle’s recent performance and strategic direction.

title: Synopsys unveils earnings beat and strong 2026 outlook
content:
Investing.com - Shares of Synopsys (NASDAQ:SNPS) were marginally lower in premarket U.S. trading on Thursday, retreating from an after-hours surge, after the engineering solutions provider topped Wall Street expectations for its fourth quarter results and issued an upbeat earnings outlook for fiscal 2026.

The chip design software provider, whose technology is used by semiconductor groups to verify processors, has been a beneficiary of a spike in popularity of artificial intelligence models which are powered by ever-more complex architectures.

California-based Synopsys has partnered with chip giants Nvidia, Intel and Qualcomm, and recently completed a purchase of simulation software firm Ansys in July.

The company reported fourth-quarter adjusted earnings per share of $2.90, exceeding analyst estimates of $2.78, while revenue reached $2.26 billion, slightly above the consensus forecast of $2.25 billion. Fourth-quarter revenue increased 37.8% versus the same period last year, with Ansys contributing $667.7 million to the total.

For the full fiscal year 2025, Synopsys posted record revenue of $7.05 billion, representing approximately 15% growth. The company’s backlog reached a $11.4 billion, which management highlighted as evidence of business resilience.

"The Synopsys team delivered a solid finish to a year that redefined our company as the leader in engineering solutions from silicon to systems," said Sassine Ghazi, president and CEO of Synopsys.

Looking ahead, Synopsys guided for annual earnings per share of $14.32 to $14.40, above the analyst consensus of $14.05. The company expects revenue between $9.56 billion and $9.66 billion, with the midpoint of $9.61 billion including approximately $2.9 billion from Ansys and reflecting the impact of roughly $110 million from divested businesses.

For the first quarter of fiscal 2026, Synopsys forecasts adjusted per-share income of $3.52 to $3.58, surpassing the consensus of $3.42, on revenue between $2.36 billion and $2.42 billion.

“Management characterized fiscal 2026 as a transitional year, especially for IP, but backlog strength supports upside potential, especially in the second half as integration and cross-sell ramp,” analysts at Stifel said in a note.

(Scott Kanowsky contributed reporting.)

title: Brookfield Asset Management stock coverage resumed by UBS with Neutral rating
content:
Investing.com - UBS has resumed coverage of Brookfield Asset Management (NYSE:BAM) with a Neutral rating and a price target of C$82.00.

The investment bank expects Brookfield to benefit from an infrastructure fundraising supercycle in 2026, with its flagship infrastructure fund in the market. UBS anticipates this fund will exceed the previous fund’s closing amount of $28 billion.

UBS also notes Brookfield’s recently launched digital infrastructure fund, which has a $10 billion target and has already secured $5 billion in commitments from NVIDIA, KIA, and Brookfield’s seed capital.

Despite these positive factors, UBS projects fee-related earnings for 2026 and 2027 that are 3% and 4% below consensus estimates. UBS points out that Brookfield’s valuation is the highest among large-cap alternative asset managers at approximately 25 times estimated 2027 earnings, reflecting its 100% fee-related earnings mix compared to peers’ 70%, a Canadian premium, and unique assets under management mix.

title: Nvidia EVP Shoquist sells $14.7 million in shares
content:
Debora Shoquist, Executive Vice President of Operations at NVIDIA CORP (NASDAQ:NVDA), sold a total of 80,000 shares of common stock on December 9, 2025, according to a Form 4 filing with the Securities and Exchange Commission. The sales amounted to approximately $14.7 million, with prices ranging from $184.2515 to $185.5467.

The filing indicates that the shares were sold in three separate transactions. 24,433 shares were sold at a weighted average price of $184.2515, 55,267 shares were sold at a weighted average price of $184.8151 and 300 shares were sold at a weighted average price of $185.5467.

Following the transactions, Shoquist indirectly owns 1,654,443 shares through the Debora C. Shoquist Revocable Living Trust and directly owns 512,644 shares of NVIDIA CORP.

The sales were executed under a pre-arranged Rule 10b5-1 trading plan adopted on September 9, 2025.

In other recent news, Nvidia’s earnings and revenue results have caught the attention of investors. The company has been involved in several significant developments, including a potential export approval for its high-end H200 AI chips to China, as noted by UBS, which maintains a Buy rating on Nvidia. Meanwhile, Nvidia has stated there is no evidence of "phantom datacenters" being used in smuggling operations, countering previous claims.

In China, the government has been holding emergency meetings with major tech firms like Alibaba, ByteDance, and Tencent to assess demand for Nvidia’s H200 chips, as reported by The Information. Additionally, Nvidia’s planned shipments of AI chips to China are set to undergo a special security review by U.S. officials, highlighting ongoing national security considerations. Hygon Information Technology has commented on the potential for intensified competition due to Nvidia’s H200 exports to China, although high purchase costs might limit market penetration.

These developments come amid broader discussions about chip exports and their implications for market dynamics.

title: Synopsys tops revenue estimates on strong demand for chip design tools
content:
Dec 10 (Reuters) - Chip design software provider Synopsys beat analysts estimates for fourth-quarter revenue on Wednesday, driven by strong demand for its chip design tools, sending its shares up 5% in extended trading.

Synopsys, whose software is used by semiconductor firms to design and verify chips, has benefited from surging investment in artificial intelligence and advanced computing, which require increasingly complex chip architectures.

It has also been benefiting from its partnership with Nvidia, Intel and Qualcomm.

Last week, Nvidia invested $2 billion in chip design software maker as part of an expanded multi-year tie-up to jointly develop new tools for designing products across industries using its AI technology.

Last month, the Sunnyvale, California-based company announced plans to lay off about 10% of its workforce, saying the move would allow it to reinvest in growth areas such as AI-driven design and system-level solutions.

Synopsys also completed its acquisition of simulation software maker Ansys in July, a deal which contributed $667.7 million in revenue for the fourth quarter. The deal, announced early last year, faced intense antitrust scrutiny in markets including Britain.

The company competes with Cadence Design Systems and Siemens in the electronic design automation market, projected to grow as chipmakers race to build AI and high-performance computing processors.

Synopsys posted quarterly revenue of $2.26 billion, above estimates of $2.25 billion according to data compiled by LSEG.

On an adjusted basis, Synopsys reported a profit of $2.90 per share, above estimates of $2.78.

For the first quarter, company sees revenue in the range of $2.36 billion to $2.42 billion, compared with analyst estimates of $2.38 billion.

title: Synopsys stock holds Buy rating at Goldman as FY2026 guidance tops EPS expectations
content:
Synopsys’ Simulation & Analysis division significantly outperformed expectations, generating $668 million in revenue compared to analyst estimates of approximately $544 million. The company also reported a substantial increase in backlog to $11.4 billion from $10.1 billion in the previous quarter.

For the first quarter of fiscal 2026, Synopsys guided revenue of $2.39 billion, slightly below consensus expectations of $2.43 billion, while projecting operating margins of 41.0% and earnings per share of $3.55, which exceeded some analyst forecasts.

The company’s full-year fiscal 2026 guidance included revenue between $9.56 billion and $9.66 billion, marginally below Street expectations, while its earnings per share guidance range of $14.32 to $14.40 came in above the consensus estimate of $14.05, supported by stronger-than-anticipated operating margin projections of 40.5%.

In other recent news, Synopsys is preparing to release its fourth-quarter fiscal 2025 results on December 10. Rosenblatt has reiterated a Buy rating with a $560 price target, expecting the company to report results in line with projections after a previous miss attributed to issues in its IP segment and the China market. BofA Securities upgraded Synopsys from Underperform to Neutral, viewing the upcoming earnings call as a potential opportunity to address past concerns, including weaknesses in its China and Intel IP business. Meanwhile, KeyBanc lowered its price target for Synopsys to $575 due to expected dilution from its investment in NVIDIA but maintained an Overweight rating. Mizuho continues to rate Synopsys as Outperform with a $600 price target, citing the benefits of an expanded partnership with Nvidia aimed at improving design and analysis processes. Additionally, Synopsys was among several semiconductor companies that met with the chairman of China’s international trade promotion body to discuss enhancing U.S.-China cooperation in the sector. These developments reflect ongoing strategic moves and market assessments ahead of Synopsys’ anticipated financial disclosures.

title: Intel pursued deals that boosted CEO Lip-Bu Tan’s fortune, sources say
content:
By Jeffrey Dastin, Max A. Cherney and Milana Vinn

SAN FRANCISCO, Dec 10 (Reuters) - When the chairman of AI chip startup Rivos wanted Intel to bid for the company, he had no need to phone the chip giant. That’s because the chairman of Rivos was also Intel’s CEO: Lip-Bu Tan.

Tan had pitched Intel’s board on buying Rivos in the summer of 2025, but he had no luck. The board told Tan he had a conflict in representing both Rivos’ interests and Intel’s, and he lacked a strategy on artificial intelligence to justify a deal, three people familiar with the events told Reuters.

Tan asked one of his lieutenants at Intel to pitch a new AI plan, leading to partnership talks with Rivos, the people said. But now there was a problem: social media giant Meta had been stalking Rivos and made an offer for the company.

Meta’s interest spurred Intel to make its own offer. Meta  countered with a sweetened bid. The competition for the startup drove the deal and incentives above the $2 billion valuation that Rivos had sought in fundraising earlier this year. Some of the sources pinned this package at around $4 billion.

Meta announced plans to buy Rivos in September. By then the bidding process had boosted the startup’s returns at Meta’s expense.

Reuters was unable to determine how much the Intel CEO profited personally as a Rivos shareholder because the financials are not public. But in a blog post on its website, Tan’s venture-capital firm, Walden Catalyst, touted how he had delivered a “successful outcome” for its investors and congratulated the Rivos team for their “remarkable achievement.”

The events show one of at least three instances where Intel has pursued deals that benefit Tan financially either by exploring bids for startups or investing in them directly through Intel’s investment arm, Intel Capital, said two of the sources.

Intel declined to make Tan available for an interview for this story. Meta did not respond to requests for comment, and Rivos declined to comment.

VENTURE CAPITALIST AS CEO

Intel hired Tan in March in part for his experience as a venture capitalist and unparalleled industry connections as a longtime investor in tech companies. Those connections have helped Intel clinch a $5 billion investment from Nvidia and a $2 billion investment from SoftBank.

Since Tan’s arrival, Intel has implemented policies requiring Tan to recuse himself from participating in investment decisions where he might benefit, two sources said. Specifically, Tan cannot attend or vote in decision meetings of Intel’s board or Intel Capital’s investment committee if he has a conflict in a venture or company-wide transaction, the sources said.

Such recusals are commonplace in industry. But they had not been an issue to the same degree at Intel because its leadership prior to Tan had fewer potentially conflicting investments, three of the sources said.

In the event of a Tan recusal for Intel Capital, the first two sources said, decision-making authority for the venture unit’s investment committee goes to Chief Financial Officer David Zinsner, who reports to Tan.

Intel declined to make Zinsner available for an interview.

Intel’s board knew when it appointed Tan that his web of investments in chip and technology companies could create conflicts, but the board accepted this, hoping that Tan can revive the iconic U.S. chipmaker, which lost $19 billion last year, one of the people said.

Intel’s 11 independent board directors did not individually comment on Tan, but in response to Reuters queries, an Intel spokesperson said: “The Board of Directors believes it’s important that Intel fully leverage his vast network and position Intel to capture the next wave of industry innovation and opportunity.”

Tan’s dealmaking comes as the administration of President Donald Trump agreed to make an $8.9 billion investment for what would be the largest ownership stake in the chipmaker, designating it as strategic to the U.S. and effectively making its citizens shareholders.

Some chip-industry analysts have said in research notes that they welcomed Tan’s industry relationships. “He has a wide view across the ecosystem,” said Bernstein analyst Stacy Rasgon in an interview with Reuters. “And that’s helpful for Intel.”

Tan does not perceive his dealmaking at Intel to be conflicted, said two of the sources, who are familiar with his thinking. Tan believes that his roles at these startups and at Intel make him uniquely able to negotiate transactions that benefit all parties, the people said.

In a statement, an Intel spokesperson disputed that Tan’s dealmaking posed any problem. “The company has an unwavering commitment to the highest standards of corporate governance, integrity, and accountability,” the spokesperson said. Tan’s “extensive relationships across the global semiconductor ecosystem are invaluable as Intel positions itself to capitalize on a rapidly evolving industry landscape.”

The Securities and Exchange Commission would not require Intel to disclose related-party transactions that could involve Tan until the spring of 2026, a year after its last disclosure, which took place the week after his start date.

U.S. regulations require such disclosures when transactions personally benefiting corporate officers exceed $120,000, though experts have said smaller dollar amounts can be material to investors if the information would inform a decision to sell or purchase stock.

DEALS TO REVIVE INTEL’S AI STRATEGY

Tan believed Intel needed to buy Rivos because earlier in-house efforts to enter the AI chip market had failed, one of the sources said. In the statement, the Intel spokesperson said Tan was “advancing its AI strategy” and “revitalizing its engineering-centric, customer-first culture.”

Rivos was one of the major targets for Intel where Tan had interests on both sides of the deal. Tan also pitched Intel’s board on buying the troubled AI computing startup SambaNova, where Tan served as executive chairman, the first three sources said.

The rationale - debated inside Intel - was that SambaNova also could provide more tech and talent to build AI chips, the sources said.

SambaNova declined to comment on any talks between the startup and Intel. “While we’re always exploring strategic options, our focus remains on accelerating the roadmap, delivering products to market, and supporting our customers,” a spokesperson said.

Intel also declined to comment on the SambaNova talks.

Tan’s portfolio has drawn scrutiny before. In April, Reuters documented how Tan’s investment firms had stakes in more than 600 Chinese companies, some with military ties, drawing a rebuke by Trump that the U.S. chip manufacturer’s CEO was "highly CONFLICTED.”

According to a White House official, Tan subsequently cleared up Trump’s concerns in an Oval Office meeting, which paved the way for collaboration on U.S. national and economic security. In September, Trump publicly celebrated Intel’s rising share price following Tan’s Nvidia deal.

Intel’s share price has roughly doubled since Tan’s appointment, outpacing the percentage gains of the S&P 500 and chip leader Nvidia in that time.

TAKING CONTROL OF INTEL CAPITAL

Soon after his appointment as CEO, Tan took direct control of Intel’s investment arm, Intel Capital, reversing a plan to spin it off. Instead, he reorganized the company so that Intel Capital would report to him, according to two of the sources, for reasons Intel has not disclosed. Its investment committee would be composed solely of Tan and one of his direct reports, finance chief Zinsner, said three people familiar with the change.

Since then, Intel Capital has invested in several companies in which Tan has a stake through his investment vehicles or venture capital firms, which include A&E Investment LLC, Celesta Capital and Walden International, three of the sources said. Some Intel staff have felt an obligation to explore such deals to win Tan’s support, two of them said.

One such investment was in proteanTecs, which announced a late-stage, Series D funding round in September. Intel Capital upped its existing stake in the startup, helping increase the value of Tan’s holdings through A&E Investment and Celesta Capital, funding data shows.

ProteanTecs declined to comment. A&E Investment, Walden Catalyst and Walden International did not respond to requests for comment. In a statement, Celesta Capital said Tan "has always acted with integrity and a commitment to doing what is right for all stakeholders.”

Before Tan became CEO, Intel Capital co-invested with him or his investment firms at least 12 times since 2019, funding announcements show.

Intel is not alone in having a venture capital arm. That’s common at large technology companies, including Nvidia, Microsoft, Alphabet and Qualcomm. But unlike those other companies, Intel is unusual in having a CEO who oversees its venture unit while also leading unrelated investment firms, a Reuters review of leadership at those companies shows.

Reuters was unable to determine how much Intel’s recent investments had in total increased Tan’s net worth, estimated to be well over $500 million.

Two corporate governance experts consulted by Reuters said Tan’s dealmaking raises red flags due to the conflicts inherent in forging deals with his own portfolio companies.

One of them also said that Intel could benefit from Tan’s connections. “You don’t want to preclude making good investments because your CEO is well connected,” said Daniel Taylor, a professor at the Wharton School specializing in corporate disclosures and insider trading.

TAN TOUTS HIS BOOK

The Intel CEO has consistently highlighted his portfolio in public appearances. In one of his first presentations as the Intel chief in March, he talked up his 251 chip-related investments, an online video from the occasion shows.

At an October chip conference in Phoenix, Tan gave a speech representing Intel that featured slides touting still more holdings, including in proteanTecs and SambaNova.

Intel’s code of conduct encourages executives to disclose potential conflicts to the company’s board and top legal and compliance officers for resolution. “We avoid situations that interfere or appear to interfere with our ability to act in the best interests of Intel,” the code of conduct states. It adds that staff must mind conflicts arising from “an ownership interest in an Intel supplier, customer, or competitor” and from “outside employment that interferes with your obligations to Intel.”

The two corporate governance experts said Tan should have dropped his portfolio investments, placed them in a blind trust or set up a special committee of the board to remove potential conflicts with his investment portfolio. Some lawyers have said special committees are not always necessary, and some scholars argue outside board roles apprise executives of strategic information.

Intel declined to say if Tan had taken any such measure.

The Intel board’s independent Audit Committee “actively monitors, reviews, and approves, as appropriate, any related-party transactions in strict accordance with Intel’s rigorous Related-Party Transactions Policy,” the company spokesperson said.

The policy has exceptions. Transactions in which an Intel executive owns less than 10% of a company that is party to a deal, whose value does not exceed $1 million or 2% of that company’s revenue, are deemed to be “pre-cleared,” the policy states. It was not clear how many investments by Intel, if any, fell under this policy, which pre-dated Tan’s leadership.

INTEL EXPLORES BID FOR A STRUGGLING STARTUP

In 2018, SambaNova was a startup with lofty ambitions to build an AI computing system that could rival Nvidia’s AI hardware and software ecosystem. Tan’s venture firm Walden International co-led SambaNova’s $56 million Series A funding round that year, which secured Tan’s position on its board.

Over the years, SambaNova hoovered up money from investors: more from Tan’s venture firm and, around 2021, a big check from SoftBank, where Tan served as a board director until 2022. The $676 million Series D round led by SoftBank valued SambaNova at around $5 billion and gave Tan’s holdings a healthy increase on paper.

SoftBank declined to answer if Tan had a role in persuading it to invest in SambaNova.

But the startup’s ambitious vision wasn’t panning out, three people familiar with the matter said. Customers had more demand for Nvidia’s chips, which are good for a wide variety of uses in AI. That contrasts with the silicon from SambaNova, which is designed for more specific AI applications.

In 2024, Tan stepped in as SambaNova’s new executive chairman in the hopes that he could help grow its business faster. The company was poised to run out of its cash, three people familiar with the matter said. It laid off 77 people in California, or reportedly about 15% of its staff, in April.

The chip startup tried to drum up interest for another funding round, but found few takers, two sources said. Its revenue meant it would have to fundraise or go up for sale at a lower valuation, said the people and a third source. Bankers pegged its worth at $2 billion to around $3 billion at most, two of the sources said.

Tan asked Intel to look at a deal with the chipmaker over the summer, three sources said.

In recent weeks, some of SambaNova’s investors provided additional financing to tide over the startup, two people familiar with the insider round said.

In a statement to Reuters, a SambaNova spokesperson confirmed the startup recently secured additional funding, adding its business “is performing really well.”

Deal talks with SambaNova are ongoing, two of the sources said. Intel and SambaNova have signed a non-binding term sheet, one of them said.

title: JPMorgan investment trusts disclose top holdings as of November 28
content:
LONDON - JPMorgan Investment Trusts has released details of the ten largest investments for each of its investment trusts as of November 28, 2025, according to a press release issued today.

The JPMorgan American Investment Trust’s portfolio shows technology companies dominating its top holdings, with Nvidia Corp leading at 7.0% of total assets, followed by Microsoft Corp at 6.2% and Broadcom Inc at 5.2%. The top ten investments represent 44.1% of the trust’s total assets.

JPMorgan Claverhouse Investment Trust, which focuses on UK equities, has HSBC Holdings Plc as its largest position at 8.0%, with Shell Plc and AstraZeneca Group Plc following at 6.5% and 5.8% respectively. The top ten holdings account for 43.3% of total assets.

The JPMorgan Emerging Markets Growth & Income PLC shows significant concentration in Asian technology, with Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Co Ltd comprising 14.8% of total assets and Tencent Holdings Ltd at 8.1%.

JPMorgan China Growth & Income PLC demonstrates the highest concentration among the trusts, with its top ten investments representing 51.4% of total assets. Tencent Holdings Ltd and Alibaba are its largest positions at 12.0% and 11.4% respectively.

Other trusts in the disclosure include JPMorgan European Discovery Trust, JPMorgan European Growth & Income, JPMorgan Global Emerging Markets Income Trust, The Mercantile Investment Trust, JPMorgan Global Growth & Income, JPMorgan Emerging Europe, Middle East & Africa Securities, JPMorgan UK Small Cap Growth & Income, JPMorgan US Smaller Companies Investment Trust, JPMorgan Japanese Investment Trust, JPMorgan India Growth & Income, and JPMorgan Asia Growth & Income.

The disclosure notes that investments in liquidity stocks are excluded from the calculations.

title: Stock market today: S&P 500 closes lower amid caution ahead of Fed decision
content:
Investing.com -- The S&P 500 closed lower Tuesday as Federal Reserve kicked off its two-day meeting, which is expected to culminate in third rate cut for the year.

At 4:0 ET p.m. (21:00 GMT), the Dow Jones Industrial Average fell 178 points, or 0.4%, and the S&P 500 index was 0.1%, while the NASDAQ Composite slipped 0.1%.

Fed meeting kicks off as widely expected rate cut decision looms

The Fed got its latest two-day policy meeting underway, which investors are expecting will end with a cut to U.S. interest rates on Wednesday.

The chances of an easing from Fed’s current target rate of 3.75%-4% now stand at roughly 89%, CME FedWatch has shown.

Still, with some policymakers recently flagging concerns around unveiling a third cut since September during a time when fresh economic data has been scarce because of a record-long government shutdown, this could prove to be one of the most contentious decision in years.

"There are now high expectations of a ’hawkish cut’ at Wednesday evening’s FOMC decision," said analysts at ING, in a note.

Data released earlier Tuesday has added to the potential that the FOMC policymakers try to play down expectations of another rate cut in the new year, as U.S. private payrolls increased by 4,750 on average per week in the four weeks ending on November 22, according to a weekly update of the monthly ADP National Employment Report.

Additionally, U.S. job openings increased marginally in October, rising 12,000 to 7.670 million, the Labor Department’s Bureau of Labor Statistics said.

The BLS is due to release its closely-monitored nonfarm payrolls report for November on December 16, after it was delayed by a record-long U.S. government shutdown. The October unemployment rate will also never be known, after having hovered around a four-year high of 4.4% in September.

Nvidia, Warner Bros Discovery in spotlight

However, most eyes are likely to be on Nvidia (NASDAQ:NVDA) after President Donald Trump stated, via a Truth Social post, that he will allow the company to sell its H200 AI chips to approved customers in China and other countries, albeit with a 25% tariff and with restrictions to ensure U.S. national security.

Trump said he had informed Chinese President Xi Jinping of his decision, to which Xi “responded positively.”

Nvidia’s H200 chip was unveiled in 2023 as the successor to the company’s H100 chip, and is estimated to be about six times more powerful than the H20, the most advanced AI chip that Nvidia is currently allowed to sell to China.

Nvidia chips are widely used by Chinese companies in AI development, with Trump’s Monday announcement presenting a path for higher chip sales for the world’s most valuable company.

Additionally, Warner Bros Discovery (NASDAQ:WBD) has another suitor, as just days after streaming titan Netflix (NASDAQ:NFLX) was named the winner of a bidding war for the studio, Paramount Skydance (NASDAQ:PSKY) said it had launched a hostile bid worth $108.4 billion.

The amount would surpass the $72 billion equity deal Netflix had secure for Warner’s television, film studios and streaming assets, and notably, Paramount’s rival bid would be for all of Warner, including its cable television properties.

Energy stocks, meanwhile, were also in the ascendency, led by Exxon Mobil Corp (NYSE:XOM) after the latter upgraded its earnings and cash flow outlook for the period spanning between 2024 and 2030.

On the earnings front, meanwhile, AutoZone Inc (NYSE:AZO) slumped 7% after reporting quarterly results that missed analyst estimates.

Toll Brothers Inc (NYSE:TOL) also came under pressure following a 3Q earnings miss.

Peter Nurse, Ambar Warrick contributed to this article

title: Supermicro launches liquid-cooled NVIDIA HGX B300 systems for AI data centers
content:
Super Micro Computer Inc. (SMCI) announced the availability of new 4U and 2-OU liquid-cooled NVIDIA HGX B300 systems designed for hyperscale data centers and AI deployments.

The 4U system is designed for standard 19-inch racks and can accommodate up to 64 GPUs per rack. The system uses Supermicro’s DLC-2 direct liquid-cooling technology, which the company states captures up to 98% of system heat.

The 2-OU system follows the 21-inch OCP Open Rack V3 specification and supports up to 144 GPUs in a single rack. A single ORV3 rack can house up to 18 nodes with 144 GPUs total, scaling with NVIDIA Quantum-X800 InfiniBand switches and Supermicro’s 1.8MW coolant distribution units.

Both systems feature eight NVIDIA Blackwell Ultra GPUs operating at up to 1,100W TDP each and include 2.1TB of HBM3e GPU memory per system. The platforms support network throughput up to 800Gb/s through integrated NVIDIA ConnectX-8 SuperNICs when used with NVIDIA networking equipment.

"We’re now offering the industry’s most compact NVIDIA HGX B300 solutions—achieving up to 144 GPUs in a single rack—while reducing power consumption and cooling costs through our proven direct liquid-cooling technology," said Charles Liang, president and CEO of Supermicro.

Supermicro states its DLC-2 technology can provide up to 40% power savings and enable 45°C warm water operation, potentially eliminating the need for chilled water and compressors in data centers.

The systems are part of Supermicro’s broader NVIDIA Blackwell portfolio, which includes NVIDIA GB300 NVL72, NVIDIA HGX B200, and NVIDIA RTX PRO 6000 Blackwell Server Edition platforms. The information is based on a company press release.

title: Nvidia’s AI chips for China to undergo special US security review - WSJ
content:
Investing.com -- The artificial-intelligence chips that Nvidia plans to ship to China will undergo a special security review in the United States before export, administration officials said, according to a report from the Wall Street Journal.

This unusual step underscores the national-security concerns facing the Trump administration following its decision to permit these controversial sales.

The Nvidia H200 AI chips included in the deal would primarily be manufactured in Taiwan. According to people familiar with the matter, these chips would then be transported to the United States for a national-security review before being forwarded to China.

Experts noted that this complex supply-chain journey and the unusual security review highlight the unprecedented nature of the agreement.

title: TSX higher with Bank of Canada, Fed meetings in focus
content:
Investing.com - Canada’s main stock exchange was higher on Tuesday, as investors geared up for a slate of key central bank decisions in the U.S. and Canada this week.

By 12.01 ET, the S&P/TSX 60 index standard futures contract had moved up by 9.3 points, or 0.51%.

The {{24441|S&P/TSX composite index} was up 187 or 0.6% at 31,357.02

On Monday index ended lower by 0.45% at 31,169.97, retreating from a record high posted on Thursday.

Still, the TSX remains up by 25% so far this year, and is on pace to log its best year since 2009.

On tap this week is a major interest rate decision from the Bank of Canada, with policymakers anticipated to keep rates on hold due to stronger-than-expected jobs figures.

U.S. stocks higher

U.S. stocks were marginally higher amid caution before the start of the Fed’s final two-day meeting for the year, which is likely to drive sentiment into the new year.

The Dow Jones Industrial Average gained 115 points, or 0.2%, and the S&P 500 index rose 4 points, or 0.1%, while the NASDAQ Composite slipped 20 points, or 0.1%.

The main averages on Wall Street slipped in the preceding session, weighed down by a climb in Treasury yields, in a sign that caution may be prevailing ahead of the Fed’s upcoming announcement on Wednesday.

Fed rate cut looms

Attention is firmly on the start of the Fed’s latest two-day policy meeting, which investors are expecting will end with a cut to U.S. interest rates on Wednesday.

Bets on a 25-basis point reduction by the U.S. central bank have increased as economic data has pointed to an American jobs market under pressure and stable -- albeit elevated -- inflation.

The chances of an easing from Fed’s current target rate of 3.75%-4% now stand at roughly 89%, CME FedWatch has shown.

Still, with some policymakers recently flagging concerns around unveiling a third cut since September during a time when fresh economic data has been scarce because of a record-long government shutdown, this could prove to be one of the most contentious decision in years.

"There are now high expectations of a ’hawkish cut’ at Wednesday evening’s FOMC decision," said analysts at ING, in a note.

The central bank’s outlook for the U.S. economy going forward will also be closely watched, especially amid heightened uncertainty over the world’s largest economy.

Nvidia, Warner Bros Discovery in spotlight

There are a few corporate earnings to digest Tuesday, including from AutoZone (NYSE:AZO), Campbell’s (NASDAQ:CPB), AeroVironment (NASDAQ:AVAV) and Cracker Barrel Old Country Store (NASDAQ:CBRL).

However, most eyes are likely to be on Nvidia (NASDAQ:NVDA) after President Donald Trump stated, via a Truth Social post, that he will allow the company to sell its H200 AI chips to approved customers in China and other countries, albeit with a 25% tariff and with restrictions to ensure U.S. national security.

Trump said he had informed Chinese President Xi Jinping of his decision, to which Xi “responded positively.”

Nvidia’s H200 chip was unveiled in 2023 as the successor to the company’s H100 chip, and is estimated to be about six times more powerful than the H20, the most advanced AI chip that Nvidia is currently allowed to sell to China.

Nvidia chips are widely used by Chinese companies in AI development, with Trump’s Monday announcement presenting a path for higher chip sales for the world’s most valuable company.

Additionally, Warner Bros Discovery (NASDAQ:WBD) has another suitor, as just days after streaming titan Netflix (NASDAQ:NFLX) was named the winner of a bidding war for the studio, Paramount Skydance (NASDAQ:PSKY) said it had launched a hostile bid worth $108.4 billion.

The amount would surpass the $72 billion equity deal Netflix had secure for Warner’s television, film studios and streaming assets, and notably, Paramount’s rival bid would be for all of Warner, including its cable television properties.

Crude stable with Ukraine talks in focus

Oil prices stabilized Tuesday after the previous session’s weakness, as traders maintain a watchful look at the peace talks to end Russia’s war in Ukraine.

Brent futures climbed 0.4% to $62.75 a barrel, and U.S. West Texas Intermediate crude futures rose 0.5% to $59.15 a barrel.

Both contracts slipped 2% on Monday after Iraq’s move to restore production at the West Qurna-2 oilfield, a site representing a meaningful share of national exports.

Ukraine has signaled it will present a revised peace proposal to the U.S. after talks in London between President Volodymyr Zelenskiy and leaders of France, Germany, and Britain.

The prospect of progress toward a negotiated settlement could remove part of the geopolitical risk premium embedded in crude prices.

Gold steadies

Gold prices advanced, as metal markets hunkered down before the Fed meeting.

The yellow metal has logged some weakness so far in December, but was sitting on four straight months of outsized gains on cheer over lower U.S. interest rates. Broader metal prices were also mostly flat on Tuesday, while silver hovered near record highs after sharp gains in the past week.

Spot gold moved up 0.3% to $4,201.65 an ounce, while gold futures for February gained slightly to $4,231.10/oz.

title: 3 China AI stocks to consider on Nvidia exports news
content:
Investing.com -- China’s AI infrastructure and cloud providers could be the early winners after the U.S. signaled it will allow exports of Nvidia’s H200 GPUs to China, according to Bank of America.

The Trump administration announced that H200 shipments will be permitted “under specific conditions, including a 25% fee per chip,” BofA analyst Daley Li noted.

The bank adds that exports will be limited to “commercial customers that successfully pass U.S. government security reviews,” while shipment timing and volumes remain uncertain.

Li highlighted the significance of the shift, stressing that Nvidia’s H200 is “more advanced than major domestic AI accelerator chips” on metrics including computing power and memory bandwidth.

If meaningful volumes ultimately reach China, the bank argues the new supply could “benefit the large-scale model training within AI clusters and inferencing for applications.”

For data centers, “improved GPU availability could drive future new order growth,” BofA writes.

Meanwhile, public cloud providers could also benefit as “additional compute capacity may support faster growth of AI-related cloud services.”

Because advanced-node domestic AI chips remain constrained by limited local fabrication capacity, the reopening of access to Nvidia hardware could directly spur investment. BofA highlights three stocks as potential beneficiaries: VNET, GDS, and Kingsoft Cloud.

For VNET and GDS, the bank sees access to H200 GPUs potentially “accelerating the placement of new data center orders.”

Meanwhile, for Kingsoft Cloud, “access to Nvidia’s H200 GPUs would enable them to expand AI computing resources,” creating “incremental revenue contributions” as customers increase model-training and inference workloads.

title: Wall St subdued at open as Fed rate cut decision nears
content:
Dec 9 (Reuters) - Wall Street’s main stock indexes were subdued at the open on Tuesday as investors awaited the Federal Reserve’s policy decision, while Nvidia shares were whipsawed by contrasting news around exports of advanced AI chips to China.

The Dow Jones Industrial Average fell 14.8 points, or 0.03%, at the open to 47,724.52. The S&P 500 fell 5.9 points, or 0.09%, at the open to 6,840.61, while the Nasdaq Composite dropped 41.3 points, or 0.18%, to 23,504.614 at the opening bell.

title: Micron’s profit outlook brightens from DRAM prices surge, HSBC starts at Buy
content:
Investing.com -- Micron Technology is set to see a sharp lift in revenue and profit as a global shortage pushes dynamic random access memory prices higher, HSBC said as it initiated stock with a Buy rating. The company will report its earnings on 17 Dec, Wednesday.

Suppliers’ DRAM inventories have tightened to two to four weeks in October from as much as 17 weeks late last year, according to TrendForce. This imbalance is expected to deepen through 2026 as demand from AI servers accelerates and supply growth remains constrained.

The bank forecasts DRAM revenue across the industry to rise 69% in 2026 and expects Micron to be one of the biggest beneficiaries due to its heavy mix of commodity DRAM.

Micron’s earnings are also set to widen as it shifts to its 1gamma (1cnm) DRAM technology, which improves bit density per wafer by more than 30%. HSBC said that step-change in density lowers cost per bit and supports margin expansion at a time when pricing momentum is already strong.

There is additional upside from the transition to next-generation high bandwidth memory, or HBM4, starting in early fiscal 2026.

HBM carries a price premium of more than 40% and is central to upcoming AI accelerators such as NVIDIA’s Rubin Ultra architecture, which is expected to lift memory content per GPU from late 2026.

Micron is also positioned to benefit from a lift in NAND demand as it raises its share in enterprise solid-state drives to 25 to 30% by fiscal 2027 from 15% in the second quarter of fiscal 2025, supported by its push into quad-level cell technology.

HSBC said AI-driven capital spending by cloud providers, including neo-cloud operators and the US Stargate project, will sustain what it sees as a four to five-year memory upcycle, longer than past cycles. Rising demand for buffer memory inside AI racks and new formats such as SOCAMM2 should extend the rally beyond commodity DRAM.

Micron shares have risen 172% this year but the stock has stalled in recent weeks on concerns over the financing capacity of some new cloud operators. According to HSBC such fears are overdone. 

Brokergae set a target price of $330, a 3x multiple to fiscal 2027 book value. The bank sees an operating profit compound annual growth rate of 125% from fiscal 2025 to 2027.

title: Nvidia stock may see China export approval for H200 chips, UBS says

Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang met with President Trump to discuss export controls, expressing both support and concerns about the current restrictions. Meanwhile, AlphaTON Capital Corp announced the successful deployment of Nvidia’s H200 GPUs on Telegram’s Cocoon AI Network, generating revenue and launching a new initiative to broaden AI infrastructure access. These recent developments present a mixed landscape of opportunities and challenges for Nvidia.

title: Will AI reshape Europe’s banks in 2026? UBS highlights top picks
content:
Investing.com -- Artificial intelligence is emerging as a central factor that could influence the performance and valuation of Europe’s banks in 2026, according to analysts at UBS in a note dated Tuesday.

The brokerage describes 2026 as the year in which markets may decide whether AI will materially transform the industry and whether banks will be among the largest beneficiaries.

UBS argues that the sector is at an early stage of what it describes as “retooling the knowledge economy,” pointing to indicators including Nvidia’s $4.4 trillion valuation and widespread adoption of generative AI tools.

UBS notes that European banks employ about 2.1 million people and currently operate with cost ratios broadly unchanged from a decade ago, meaning efficiency gains could be significant if technology is applied at scale.

UBS cites analysis from McKinsey estimating potential cost reductions of 15% to 20% through AI, which it calculates would equate to about 20% more profit before tax.

However, the brokerage states that banks are not yet showing improvements in efficiency metrics and that implementation will take years. Even so, UBS says the possibility of such an earnings shift is large enough to influence valuations.

The analysts note that bank stocks delivered a 66% total return in 2025 compared with 18% for the broader European equity market, aided by earnings upgrades and valuation expansion.

Despite that rally, banks still trade at a 36% price-to-earnings discount to the wider market on forward estimates. UBS argues that AI-related upside, along with earnings momentum and M&A activity, means the sector remains attractively valued.

UBS identifies ABN Amro and Erste as new top picks, replacing Bawag, Intesa and Standard Chartered after strong performance in 2025.

Other highlighted names include Barclays, ING, Piraeus, Santander and Société Générale. The brokerage notes potential implied returns in 2025 ranging from 14% to 33% based on price targets and dividend yields, led by Piraeus with 28% upside and a 5.2% dividend yield.

UBS states that 2026 may bring greater investor focus on structural earnings growth and return on equity differentiation.

It also highlights AI as a source of “potential upside to near term valuations and longer term earnings,” alongside contributing factors such as sector M&A and stabilizing interest income trends.

UBS adds that broad adoption of AI tools is visible in sectors such as audit, law and consulting, but banks have yet to deliver measurable efficiency benefits.

It frames 2026 as an inflection point in which markets may decide whether AI represents meaningful profit potential or remains speculative.

title: CoreWeave expands Mission Control platform with new AI monitoring tools
content:
CoreWeave Inc. (CRWV) announced expanded functionality for its Mission Control platform, adding new capabilities designed to help enterprises monitor and manage large-scale AI workloads.

The cloud computing company said the enhanced platform includes three new features: Telemetry Relay, GPU Straggler Detection, and Mission Control Agent. These additions provide enterprises with visibility into system access events and help identify performance bottlenecks in distributed training operations.

Telemetry Relay streams audit and access logs from CoreWeave services to customer security information and event management systems or observability tools. The service supports multi-destination routing and includes service level objectives for delivery reliability.

GPU Straggler Detection offers rank-level visibility inside distributed training jobs to identify specific GPUs or nodes causing performance issues. The feature integrates with existing observability tools and uses NVIDIA Collective Communications Library signals.

The Mission Control Agent functions as a conversational assistant that allows teams to interact with the platform directly for system troubleshooting and guidance.

"Mission Control gives enterprises the first true operating standard for AI at production scale," said Peter Salanki, co-founder and chief technology officer of CoreWeave. "It unifies the entire stack so every layer is visible, every issue is surfaced early, and every insight is actionable."

Grafana Labs announced a formal partnership with CoreWeave on the Mission Control platform. "By combining CoreWeave’s high-performance AI cloud with Grafana’s enterprise-grade observability platform, organizations can troubleshoot with precision and maintain reliability at massive scale," said Ash Mazhari, vice president of corporate development at Grafana Labs.

The expanded Mission Control is available across CoreWeave’s AI Cloud platform. Telemetry Relay is generally available, while GPU Straggler Detection and Mission Control Agent are in preview status.

title: Marvell stock rating reiterated at Overweight by JPMorgan on strong AI outlook
content:
Investing.com - JPMorgan has reiterated an Overweight rating and $130.00 price target on Marvell (NASDAQ:MRVL) following a meeting with the company’s President and COO Chris Koopmans and SVP of IR Ashish Saran. This target represents significant upside from the current price of $92, aligning with the strong analyst consensus of 1.77 (Buy) and high target of $156.

The company confirmed it has secured purchase orders for all of 2026 for the next-generation Trainium 3 XPU ASIC program at AWS, while Microsoft’s 3nm Maia AI XPU ASIC program remains on track to ramp in the second half of 2026 and into 2027. Marvell is already working on next-generation 2nm XPU programs for both customers. As a prominent player in the Semiconductors industry with a market cap of $78.03 billion, Marvell is well-positioned to capitalize on these partnerships.

The company remains the primary supplier for all initial 1.6Tbps DSP deployments and has started shipping 100G/200G per lane AEC DSP solutions, which management expects to at least double next year from approximately $100 million in the current year.

Marvell is engaged with multiple cloud providers and hyperscalers on scale-up networking opportunities, which will begin contributing to 2027 revenues as customers transition to rack-scale solutions, with the company positioned to capture a significant portion of what it projects will be a $16 billion market by 2030.

In other recent news, Marvell has been in the spotlight with several analyst updates and financial outlooks. The company recently provided guidance showing sequential revenue acceleration throughout next year, which has influenced analysts’ perspectives. Piper Sandler raised its price target on Marvell to $135 from $85, maintaining an Overweight rating, citing visibility and orders supporting long-term guidance. Similarly, Benchmark increased its price target to $130 from $95, maintaining a Buy rating, following Marvell’s latest financial outlook. However, Benchmark also downgraded Marvell’s stock rating from Buy to Hold due to concerns about losing Amazon’s Tranium 3 and 4 designs to competitor Alchip. Meanwhile, TD Cowen raised its price target to $100 from $90, maintaining a Hold rating, based on increased confidence in Marvell’s fiscal 2026 outlook. JPMorgan reaffirmed its Overweight rating, dismissing concerns about potential share losses in AI chip engagements with Microsoft and Amazon. These developments reflect the varied analyst opinions on Marvell’s future performance and market position.

title: US to allow Nvidia H200 chip shipments to China, Trump says
content:
By Stephen Nellis, Karen Freifeld and Michael Martina

WASHINGTON, Dec 8 (Reuters) - The United States will allow Nvidia’s H200 processors, its second-best artificial intelligence chips, to be exported to China and collect a 25% fee on such sales, U.S. President Donald Trump said on Monday.

The decision appears to settle a U.S. debate about whether Nvidia and rivals should maintain their global lead in AI chips by selling to China or withhold the exports, though Beijing has told companies not to use U.S. technology, leaving it unclear whether Trump’s decision would lead to new sales.

Nvidia shares rose 2% in after-hours trading after Trump made the announcement on Truth Social, following a 3% rise during the day on a report by Semafor.

Trump said in his post that he had informed President Xi Jinping of China, where Nvidia’s chips are under government scrutiny, about the move and that he "responded positively."

He said the U.S. Commerce Department was finalizing details of the arrangement and the same approach would apply to other AI chip firms such as Advanced Micro Devices and Intel.

Trump’s post said the fee to be paid to the U.S. government was "$25%", and a White House official confirmed he meant 25%, higher than the 15% proposed in August.

"We will protect National Security, create American Jobs, and keep America’s lead in AI," Trump wrote on Truth Social. "NVIDIA’s U.S. Customers are already moving forward with their incredible, highly advanced Blackwell chips, and soon, Rubin, neither of which are part of this deal."

Trump did not say how many H200 chips would be authorized for shipment or what conditions might apply, only that exports would occur "under conditions that allow for continued strong National Security."

Administration officials consider the move a compromise between sending Nvidia’s latest Blackwell chips to China, which Trump has declined to allow, and sending China no U.S. chips at all, which officials believe would bolster Huawei’s efforts to sell AI chips in China, a person familiar with the matter said.

"Offering H200 to approved commercial customers, vetted by the Department of Commerce, strikes a thoughtful balance that is great for America," Nvidia said in a statement.

Intel declined to comment. The U.S. Commerce Department, which oversees export controls, and AMD did not respond to requests for comment.

A White House official said that the 25% fee would be collected as an import tax from Taiwan, where the chips are made, to the United States, where the chips will undergo a security review by U.S. officials before being exported to China.

Asked about the approval on Tuesday, the Chinese foreign ministry said that China believes it should cooperate with the U.S. to achieve mutual benefits.

FEARS OF CHIPS STRENGTHENING CHINA’S MILITARY

China hawks in Washington are concerned that selling more advanced AI chips to China could help Beijing supercharge its military, fears that had first prompted limits on such exports by the Biden administration.

The Trump administration had been considering greenlighting the sale, sources told Reuters last month. Trump said last week he met with Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang and that the executive was aware of where he stood on export controls.

"It’s a terrible mistake to trade off national security for advantages in trade," said Eric Hirschhorn, who was a senior Commerce Department official during the Obama administration. "It cuts against the consistent policies of Democratic and Republican administrations alike not to assist China’s military modernization."

According to a report released on Sunday by the non-partisan think tank, the Institute for Progress (IFP), the H200 would be almost six times as powerful as the H20, the most advanced AI semiconductor that can legally be exported to China, after the Trump administration reversed its short-lived ban on such sales this year.

The Blackwell chip now in use by U.S. AI firms is about 1.5 times faster than H200 chips for training AI systems, the IFP said, and five times faster for inferencing work where AI models are put to use. Nvidia’s own research has suggested Blackwell chips are 10 times faster than H200 chips for some tasks.

Several Democratic U.S. senators in a statement described Trump’s decision as a "colossal economic and national security failure" that would be a boon to China’s industry and military.

Republican Representative John Moolenaar, who chairs the House China Select Committee, said in a statement to Reuters that China would use the chips to strengthen its military capabilities and surveillance.

"Nvidia should be under no illusions - China will rip off its technology, mass-produce it themselves and seek to end Nvidia as a competitor," he said.

In recent months, Beijing has cautioned Chinese tech companies against buying chips that Nvidia downgraded to sell to the Chinese market, which are the H20, RTX 6000D and L20, two sources said.

George Chen, partner at consultancy The Asia Group, said he expected Chinese regulators to soften their hardline approach to Nvidia following Trump’s comments on Xi’s reaction, given efforts to improve U.S.-China relations. The H200 was also far more useful to China than the H20, he said.

"I expect state media to gradually change their narrative and be more welcoming to Nvidia," he said.

Bo Zhengyuan, political analyst at consultancy firm Plenum, said he also believed that Beijing would be more cautious about intervening.

"But on a longer horizon, we don’t know how long this window can last. China will not be disturbed by this easing, and it will remain ultra-focused on gaining advanced chip-making capability of its own," he said.

China’s domestic AI chip companies now include tech giant Huawei Technologies as well as smaller players such as Cambricon and Moore Threads.

title: Morning Bid: Wobbling bonds find a level as Fed meets
content:
By Mike Dolan

Dec 9 - What matters in U.S. and global markets today

By Mike Dolan, Editor-At-Large, Finance and Markets

Fed week got off to a rocky start in global markets, courtesy of bond market hits across the world, but government debt yields stabilized on Tuesday as the Federal Reserve started its two-day meeting.

A sharp jump in yields on U.S. Treasuries, German bunds and Japanese government bonds on Monday was fueled by a slew of factors - some hawkish policy noises from several regions, another heavy week of year-end U.S. debt sales and a likely Bank of Japan interest rate rise next week.

The jolt knocked stock markets back and the S&P 500 ended in the red on Monday. Stock index futures held steady on Tuesday.

But as a widely expected Fed cut is expected on Wednesday, the picture calmed a bit overnight. The release of October data on U.S. job openings later is likely the last significant labor market update before the central bank decides, with some $39 billion of 10-year Treasury notes under the hammer today too.

The latest shakeout in global bonds on Monday was worldwide.

European Central Bank board hawk Isabel Schnabel helped push long-dated German yields to 14-year highs by saying the next move in ECB rate will eventually be higher, Fed Chair hopeful Kevin Hassett pointedly did not call for sharp interest rate cuts as he has done previously and the Reserve Bank of Australia overnight ruled out further rate cuts there.

Currencies remained relatively steady, however. The dollar was a touch firmer against the yen and euro overnight, but softer against China’s yuan and the Aussie dollar.

Back on Wall Street, the focus was on the bidding war for Warner Bros. Discovery, with Paramount Skydance launching a hostile move worth $108 billion in a last-ditch effort to outbid Netflix. Shares of Paramount were up 7.3% on Monday, Warner Bros Discovery rose 5.3% and Netflix fell 4%.

In an interesting week for Big Tech, with Oracle and Broadcom results due, AI chip giant Nvidia rose 2% out of hours after President Donald Trump said the United States will allow its H200 processors, its second-best AI chips, to be exported to China and collect a 25% fee on such sales.

But the European Commission said on Tuesday that Alphabet’s Google faces an EU antitrust investigation into its use of web publishers’ online content and YouTube videos to train its AI models.

In today’s column, I take a look at how hawks within the European Central Bank are pointing to a new threat: passive easing.        And listen to the latest episode of the new Morning Bid daily podcast. Subscribe to hear Mike and other Reuters journalists discuss the biggest news in markets and finance seven days a week.

Today’s Market Minute  * Paramount Skydance on Monday launched a hostile bid worth$108.4 billion for Warner Bros Discovery, in a last-ditch effortto outbid Netflix and create a media powerhouse that wouldchallenge the dominance of the streaming giant. * The United States will allow Nvidia’s H200 processors, itssecond-best artificial intelligence chips, to be exported toChina and collect a 25% fee on such sales, U.S. President DonaldTrump said on Monday. * Australia is set to become the first country to implementa minimum age for social media use on Wednesday, with platformslike Instagram, TikTok and YouTube forced to block more than amillion accounts, marking the beginning of an expected globalwave of regulation. * China produces more than half of the world’s steel andaluminium. But in an effort to rein in overcapacity, Beijing hasset informal production ceilings, which both sectors are likelyto brush up against, writes ROI Asia Commodities Columnist ClydeRussell. * For the first time, Texas’ main power system, the largestin the U.S., looks set to generate more power from solar farmsthan coal plants during a calendar year. Read more about Texas’sclean power breakthrough in ROI Energy Transition ColumnistGavin Maguire’s new piece.

Chart of the day

Chinese exports to the United States dropped 29% year-on-year in November, while exports to the European Union grew an annual 14.8%. Shipments to Australia surged 35.8%, and the fast-growing Southeast Asian economies took in 8.2% more goods over the same period. Tumbling exports to the U.S. came despite news that the world’s two biggest economies had agreed to scale back some of their tariffs after U.S. President Trump and Chinese President Xi Jinping met in South Korea in late October. The average U.S. tariff on Chinese goods stands at 47.5%, well above the 40% threshold that economists say erodes Chinese exporters’ profit margins.

Today’s events to watch

* U.S. NFIB November small business survey (06:00 EDT), US October JOLTS job openings data (10:00 EDT); Mexico November inflation (07:00 EDT)

* U.S. Federal Reserve’s Federal Open Market Committee starts two-day meeting, decision Wednesday

* Bank of England policymakers Clare Lombardelli, Dave Ramsden, Swati Dhingra and Catherine Mann speaking in parliament

* U.S. Treasury sells $39 billion of 10-year notes

* U.S. corporate earnings: AutoZone, Campbell’s
